---
title: "Three kernels, forward and back"
---

The [previous chapter](differentiation.qmd) argued that differentiation is linear
algebra: the derivative is a linear map, the JVP applies it, the VJP transposes it,
and nothing is ever materialised that does not have to be. This chapter cashes that
in on three workhorse kernels, chosen because each teaches a distinct lesson and
each is load-bearing in `nitrix`.

- **gemm** — the matrix multiply — shows the happiest case: its backward pass is
  *two more matrix multiplies*, the same compute-bound primitive the
  [hardware chapter](hardware.qmd#sec-tensorcores) said the tensor cores exist for.
  This is why deep networks of linear layers are cheap to train.
- **softmax** shows a *structured* Jacobian — a diagonal minus a rank-one term —
  that you must never form, only apply, and that demands numerical care.
- the **semiring** matrix product shows that the kernel itself generalises:
  replace $(+,\times)$ with another algebra and you get shortest paths, or
  log-space marginalisation, from the *same* contraction — and what
  "differentiate" then *means* generalises with it, connecting directly back to
  softmax and forward to the [inductive-bias chapter](inductive-bias.qmd).

Throughout we work in the [index notation](differentiation.qmd#sec-einstein) of the
last chapter, and we keep to its discipline: derive the JVP (pushforward), then the
VJP (pullback) as its transpose, never building a Jacobian.

## gemm: the matrix multiply {#sec-gemm}

The forward operation is the ordinary product $C = AB$ of $A\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times
k}$ and $B\in\mathbb{R}^{k\times n}$, which in index notation is a single
contraction over the shared dimension,
$$
C_{ij} \;=\; A_{ik}\,B_{kj}\qquad(\text{sum over } k),
$$
the work the tensor cores accelerate (@fig-gemm). It is *bilinear* — linear in $A$
for fixed $B$, and linear in $B$ for fixed $A$ — and that is the only fact we need
to differentiate it.

::: {#fig-gemm}
<svg viewBox="0 0 760 260" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img"
     aria-label="The matrix product C = A B contracts over the shared index k; its backward pass is two more matrix products."
     font-family="system-ui, sans-serif" font-size="13">
  <rect x="40"  y="40" width="130" height="90" rx="6" fill="#dfe7f0" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="105" y="88" text-anchor="middle">A  (m×k)</text>
  <rect x="210" y="40" width="130" height="90" rx="6" fill="#fbf0d6" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="275" y="88" text-anchor="middle">B  (k×n)</text>
  <text x="372" y="90" font-size="16">=</text>
  <rect x="400" y="40" width="130" height="90" rx="6" fill="#dce9dd" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="465" y="88" text-anchor="middle">C  (m×n)</text>
  <text x="620" y="74" text-anchor="middle">forward: 1 gemm</text>
  <text x="620" y="98" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#444b53">Cᵢⱼ = Σₖ Aᵢₖ Bₖⱼ</text>
  <rect x="40" y="168" width="680" height="62" rx="6" fill="#f4f6f8" stroke="#33373d"/>
  <text x="60" y="192" font-weight="bold">backward: two more gemms</text>
  <text x="60" y="216">Ā = C̄ Bᵀ          B̄ = Aᵀ C̄</text>
  <text x="470" y="216" font-size="11" fill="#444b53">(same compute-bound primitive, run twice)</text>
</svg>

The matrix product contracts over the shared index $k$. Because it is bilinear, its
pushforward is the product rule and its pullback is two more products of the same
kind — so a layer's forward and backward passes are all matrix multiplies, the
operation the tensor cores are built for.
:::

**JVP (pushforward).** Linearising a bilinear map is the product rule: perturb $A$ by
$\dot A$ and $B$ by $\dot B$ and the first-order change in $C$ is the sum of the two
one-sided changes,
$$
\dot C \;=\; \dot A\,B \;+\; A\,\dot B,\qquad
\dot C_{ij} = \dot A_{ik}B_{kj} + A_{ik}\dot B_{kj}.
$$
Two matrix products, no Jacobian.

**VJP (pullback).** We want the transpose: given a cotangent $\bar C$ on the output
(same shape as $C$), find the sensitivities $\bar A,\bar B$ on the inputs. By
definition the pullback satisfies $\langle\bar C,\dot C\rangle = \langle\bar A,\dot
A\rangle + \langle\bar B,\dot B\rangle$ for every perturbation, where
$\langle U,V\rangle=\operatorname{tr}(U^\top V)$ is the elementwise inner product.
Substitute the JVP and regroup each term onto its perturbation:
$$
\langle\bar C,\dot A B\rangle = \langle \bar C B^\top,\dot A\rangle,
\qquad
\langle\bar C, A\dot B\rangle = \langle A^\top\bar C,\dot B\rangle,
$$
(each step is the cyclic property of the trace), and read off the sensitivities:
$$
\boxed{\;\bar A = \bar C\,B^\top,\qquad \bar B = A^\top\,\bar C.\;}
$$
In index notation $\bar A_{ik}=\bar C_{ij}B_{kj}$ and $\bar B_{kj}=A_{ik}\bar
C_{ij}$ — the *same* indices as the forward contraction, summed on the other leg,
exactly as the [transpose rule](differentiation.qmd#sec-vjp) demanded. The lesson is
the one in the figure: **the backward pass of a matmul is two matmuls.** A linear
layer therefore costs about three times its forward pass to train, all of it the
compute-bound, tensor-core-friendly primitive — the single fact that makes deep
learning affordable.

@fig-gemm-check confirms the formulas against finite differences, in pure NumPy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gemm-check
#| fig-cap: "The gemm JVP and VJP, verified against finite differences. The pushforward Ċ = ȦB + AḂ and the pullback Ā = C̄Bᵀ, B̄ = AᵀC̄ each match a numerical reference to roughly machine precision (the bars are the max absolute error, ~1e-9 in float64), confirming the boxed formulas are exact, not approximate."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
m, k, n = 4, 5, 3
A = rng.standard_normal((m, k)); B = rng.standard_normal((k, n))
dA = rng.standard_normal((m, k)); dB = rng.standard_normal((k, n))
Cbar = rng.standard_normal((m, n))

# JVP (analytic) vs finite difference of C(A,B) = A @ B.
jvp = dA @ B + A @ dB
eps = 1e-6
jvp_fd = ((A + eps * dA) @ (B + eps * dB) - A @ B) / eps
err_jvp = np.max(np.abs(jvp - jvp_fd))

# VJP (analytic): Abar = Cbar Bᵀ, Bbar = Aᵀ Cbar. Check the adjoint identity
# <Cbar, JVP> == <Abar, dA> + <Bbar, dB> to machine precision.
Abar, Bbar = Cbar @ B.T, A.T @ Cbar
lhs = np.sum(Cbar * (dA @ B + A @ dB))
rhs = np.sum(Abar * dA) + np.sum(Bbar * dB)
err_vjp = abs(lhs - rhs)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 3.4))
ax.bar(["JVP vs\nfinite diff", "VJP adjoint\nidentity"], [err_jvp, err_vjp],
       color=["#2c6a45", "#8a3ffc"])
ax.set_yscale("log"); ax.set_ylabel("max absolute error (log)")
ax.set_title("gemm forward/backward formulas are exact")
ax.set_ylim(1e-16, 1e-4)
for i, v in enumerate([err_jvp, err_vjp]):
    ax.text(i, v * 2, f"{v:.1e}", ha="center", fontsize=8)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## softmax: a structured Jacobian {#sec-softmax}

The softmax turns a vector of scores into a probability distribution,
$$
s_i \;=\; \frac{e^{x_i}}{\sum_k e^{x_k}} .
$$
In practice it is computed in the numerically stable form $s_i = e^{x_i-c}/\sum_k
e^{x_k-c}$ with $c=\max_k x_k$: subtracting the max changes nothing mathematically
(the constant cancels) but keeps every exponential in $[0,1]$, avoiding overflow.
This **log-sum-exp** trick recurs across `nitrix` — in attention, and in the LOG
semiring below — and is the standard way to keep exponentials honest.

The Jacobian has a clean closed form. Differentiating the quotient,
$$
J_{ij} \;=\; \frac{\partial s_i}{\partial x_j} \;=\; s_i(\delta_{ij}-s_j)
\;=\; \operatorname{diag}(s) - s\,s^\top ,
$$
a **diagonal minus a rank-one outer product** ($\delta_{ij}$ is 1 when $i=j$, else
0). @fig-softmax-jac shows the structure for a small example. Two features matter.
First, it is *dense* — $m\times m$ — so for a length-$1000$ softmax the explicit
Jacobian is a million entries you never want to store. Second, it is *symmetric*
($\operatorname{diag}(s)$ and $ss^\top$ both are), which will make the pushforward
and pullback identical.

```{python}
#| label: fig-softmax-jac
#| fig-cap: "The softmax Jacobian J = diag(s) − s sᵀ for a length-8 input, as a heatmap. It is dense and symmetric: a positive diagonal s_i(1−s_i) and negative off-diagonals −s_i s_j (raising one logit lowers the others' probabilities). You never build this m×m matrix — the JVP and VJP apply it in O(m) from s and a single weighted sum."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
x = rng.standard_normal(8)
s = np.exp(x - x.max()); s /= s.sum()
J = np.diag(s) - np.outer(s, s)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.8, 4.0))
im = ax.imshow(J, cmap="RdBu_r", vmin=-np.abs(J).max(), vmax=np.abs(J).max())
ax.set_title(r"softmax Jacobian  $J = \mathrm{diag}(s) - s\,s^\top$")
ax.set_xlabel("input index j"); ax.set_ylabel("output index i")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.85, label=r"$\partial s_i/\partial x_j$")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**JVP (pushforward).** Apply $J$ to a perturbation $\dot x$ without forming it:
$$
\dot s = J\dot x = s\odot\dot x - s\,(s^\top\dot x),\qquad
\dot s_i = s_i\Bigl(\dot x_i - \textstyle\sum_k s_k\dot x_k\Bigr),
$$
where $\odot$ is the elementwise product. Read it as: scale the perturbation by the
probabilities, then subtract the probability-weighted *mean* perturbation — a
"centre, then reweight" that costs $O(m)$, not $O(m^2)$. The single scalar
$\sum_k s_k\dot x_k$ is the only thing that couples the coordinates.

**VJP (pullback).** Because $J$ is symmetric, $J^\top=J$, so the pullback is the
*same* operation applied to the cotangent $\bar y$:
$$
\bar x = J\bar y = s\odot\bar y - s\,(s^\top\bar y),\qquad
\bar x_i = s_i\Bigl(\bar y_i - \textstyle\sum_k s_k\bar y_k\Bigr).
$$
One subtracted weighted-mean, one elementwise scale — again $O(m)$. The symmetry of
the structured Jacobian is a small gift the chapter's discipline hands you for free:
forward and backward are one routine. This matrix-free, stable softmax is the
core of `nitrix`'s `nn.attention`, where an explicit per-row $m\times m$ Jacobian
would be ruinous.

## The semiring matrix product {#sec-semiring}

The third kernel generalises the *operation itself*. A matrix product does two
things — it **multiplies** pairs of entries and **sums** the results — and there is
nothing sacred about $(\times,+)$. Replace them with any pair $(\otimes,\oplus)$ that
behaves enough like multiply-and-add (a **semiring**) and the same contraction
$$
C_{ij} \;=\; \bigoplus_k\bigl(A_{ik}\otimes B_{kj}\bigr)
$$
computes something new. This is not a curiosity: it is `nitrix`'s **core
substrate**. Mesh and graph operators, morphology, and smoothing are all semiring
reductions over the sparse ELL storage of the geometry unit — "there is no
message-passing base class," only this product over a chosen algebra. The kernel is
`semiring_matmul`, and it ships with the algebras in the table below.

| Semiring | $\oplus$ (combine) | $\otimes$ (extend) | computes | backward pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **REAL** | $+$ | $\times$ | ordinary matrix product | two gemms (above) |
| **LOG** | $\log\!\sum e$ | $+$ | log-space sum-of-products (marginals) | **softmax**-weighted routing |
| **TROPICAL** | $\min$ (or $\max$) | $+$ | shortest / longest paths | **argmin** selection (a backtrace) |
| **BOOLEAN** | $\lor$ | $\land$ | reachability / connectivity | (non-differentiable) |

The striking part is what happens when we ask for the *derivative* of a semiring
product. The matrix-free pushforward/pullback machinery of the
[last chapter](differentiation.qmd) still applies — but **what the local
linearisation is depends on the algebra**, and the three differentiable cases are
exactly the previous two kernels in disguise.

- **REAL** is the gemm of [the first section](#sec-gemm): product rule forward, two
  gemms backward.
- **LOG** uses $a\oplus b=\log(e^a+e^b)$ and $a\otimes b=a+b$, so $C_{ij}=
  \operatorname{logsumexp}_k(A_{ik}+B_{kj})$. Its derivative is governed by the
  identity from the softmax section — *the gradient of log-sum-exp is the softmax*:
  $$
  \frac{\partial}{\partial u_k}\operatorname{logsumexp}(u) \;=\;
  \frac{e^{u_k}}{\sum_{k'} e^{u_{k'}}} \;=\; \operatorname{softmax}(u)_k .
  $$
  So the backward pass of a LOG-semiring product **routes the output cotangent back
  to the inputs weighted by a softmax** over the combined terms $A_{ik}+B_{kj}$ —
  the forward pass sums in log space, the backward pass distributes by the
  responsibilities. (This is the gradient of the forward algorithm, and of any
  log-partition.)
- **TROPICAL** uses $a\oplus b=\min(a,b)$, so $C_{ij}=\min_k(A_{ik}+B_{kj})$ — a
  Bellman shortest-path step. The minimum is piecewise linear; its (sub)gradient is a
  hard *selection* of the winning $k$, so the backward pass sends the cotangent
  entirely to the term that achieved the minimum. That is precisely **backtracking
  the optimal path** (the Viterbi/shortest-path traceback).

These last two are the same object at two temperatures, which is the punchline that
links this chapter to the next. Log-sum-exp is a **smooth relaxation of the
maximum** (equivalently softmin of the minimum): with an inverse-temperature
$\beta$,
$$
\tfrac{1}{\beta}\operatorname{logsumexp}(\beta\,u) \;\xrightarrow{\ \beta\to\infty\ }\;
\max_k u_k ,
$$
and the softmax weights sharpen to a one-hot argmax. So the **LOG semiring is the
differentiable relaxation of the TROPICAL one**: smoothing the hard $\min$/$\max$
into a soft, temperature-controlled average turns a combinatorial operation with a
selection "gradient" into a genuinely smooth one whose gradient is a softmax.
@fig-semiring shows the relaxation.

```{python}
#| label: fig-semiring
#| fig-cap: "The LOG semiring as a smooth relaxation of the TROPICAL one. Two candidate path-costs cross as a parameter t varies; the tropical min (black, a kink) is non-differentiable at the crossing, while the log-semiring soft-min −(1/β)·logsumexp(−β·costs) (coloured) is smooth everywhere and approaches the hard min as the inverse-temperature β grows. Its gradient is a softmax over the candidates — the differentiable stand-in for the argmin backtrace."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = np.linspace(-2, 2, 400)
cost_a = 0.5 * t + 1.0          # candidate path A cost
cost_b = -0.7 * t + 1.2         # candidate path B cost
costs = np.stack([cost_a, cost_b], axis=0)

hard_min = costs.min(axis=0)
def soft_min(beta):
    return -1.0 / beta * np.logaddexp(-beta * costs[0], -beta * costs[1])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 3.8))
ax.plot(t, cost_a, "--", color="0.7", lw=1, label="path A cost")
ax.plot(t, cost_b, ":", color="0.7", lw=1, label="path B cost")
for beta, col in [(1.0, "#3b6fb0"), (3.0, "#d08a2c"), (12.0, "#b03b4b")]:
    ax.plot(t, soft_min(beta), color=col, lw=2, label=fr"soft-min (β={beta:g})")
ax.plot(t, hard_min, color="k", lw=2.2, label="tropical min (hard)")
ax.set_xlabel("parameter t"); ax.set_ylabel("combined cost")
ax.set_title("LOG semiring smoothly relaxes the TROPICAL min")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, ncol=2, loc="upper center")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## What this means for `nitrix`

The three kernels are a tour of the chapter's one discipline — *exploit the
Jacobian's structure, never build it* — across the operations the library is made
of.

- **gemm's two-matmul backward** is why every dense layer, attention projection, and
  linear readout in `nitrix`'s `nn` seam backpropagates at the cost of a couple of
  forward passes, on the tensor cores the [hardware chapter](hardware.qmd) described.
- **softmax's structured, symmetric, matrix-free Jacobian** is what makes attention
  differentiable without a per-row $m\times m$ matrix, and its log-sum-exp form is
  the numerical-stability pattern reused wherever exponentials appear.
- **the semiring product** is the substrate beneath the geometry, graph, morphology,
  and smoothing units, and its differentiation is the unifying thread: the REAL
  backward is two gemms, the LOG backward is a softmax-weighted routing, the
  TROPICAL backward is an argmin backtrace, and the first two are one operation at
  two temperatures. The fused `semiring_matmul` kernels register exactly these as
  their `custom_vjp`, so the substrate stays differentiable.

The through-line is [Thread B](index.qmd#sec-thread-linear) meeting
[Thread C](index.qmd#sec-thread-structure): the derivative is a linear map you apply
matrix-free by exploiting its structure (diagonal, rank-one, bilinear, semiring),
and the *algebra* you choose — the structure you build in — determines both what the
kernel computes and what its gradient is. The relaxation that made the TROPICAL
semiring differentiable is the first glimpse of the [inductive-bias
chapter](inductive-bias.qmd): smoothing a discrete operation into a differentiable
one is how hard, combinatorial structure is admitted into a differentiable program.

## Further reading

- Giles, M. B. (2008). *Collected matrix derivative results for forward and reverse
  mode algorithmic differentiation.* — the matrix calculus behind the gemm JVP/VJP
  (and many other linear-algebra primitives).
- Blondel, M., Mensch, A. & Vert, J.-P. (2020). "Differentiable dynamic
  programming for structured prediction and attention." *ICML*. — the LOG-semiring
  smoothing of $\min$/$\max$ and its softmax/softargmax gradients.
- Mensch, A. & Blondel, M. (2018). "Differentiable dynamic programming." — the
  smoothed shortest-path / Viterbi view of the tropical-vs-log relaxation.
- Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y. & Courville, A. (2016). *Deep Learning*, ch. 6. — the
  softmax, log-sum-exp stability, and the backward pass of linear layers.

## See also

- The semiring substrate: [`nitrix.semiring`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/tree/main/src/nitrix/semiring)
  and the fused kernel
  [`_kernels/cuda/semiring_matmul.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/_kernels/cuda/semiring_matmul.py)
  (with its `custom_vjp`), used across the
  [geometry operator algebra](../geometry/operators.qmd).
- The softmax in action: the attention kernel
  [`_kernels/cuda/attention.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/_kernels/cuda/attention.py).
- Previous chapter: [differentiation as linear algebra](differentiation.qmd) — the
  JVP/VJP machinery these kernels instantiate. Next chapter:
  [the differentiable-programming paradigm](differentiable-programming.qmd) — how
  kernels like these compose into a program you can optimise.
